title: UK withdraws millions in funding from world’s second-largest rainforest in Congo  - Carbon Brief
author: Josh Gabbatiss
gist: The UK has abandoned roughly half of its £200m COP26 pledge to protect the Congo basin rainforest, cutting the flagship Congo Basin Forest Action Programme's budget by 79% (from £90m to £18.8m) and closing a £12.3m biodiverse landscapes project five years early, as aid cuts to fund UK defence spending collide with prior climate-finance commitments; only £39.8m of the £200m had been delivered as of 2024.

themes.theme: Scale of UK Congo funding retreat

insights.claim: The UK's Congo Basin Forest Action Programme -- explicitly designed to deliver roughly half of the UK's £200m COP26 Congo pledge -- has had its budget cut from £90m to £18.8m (a 79% reduction), of which £15m has already been spent, leaving little capacity for further disbursement.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified funding cut that materially reduces available capital for Congo basin forest conservation, a negative signal for forest-carbon credit supply and biodiversity-linked project financing dependent on UK aid.

insights.claim: The Congo portion of the UK's biodiverse landscapes fund, worth at least £12.3m and championed by the previous Conservative government, has been closed just two years into its planned seven-year schedule.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Demonstrates cross-party continuity of UK aid retrenchment (both Conservative-originated and Labour-executed cuts), signaling durable rather than transient funding risk for Congo conservation programs.

insights.claim: As of 2024, the UK had spent only £39.8m of its £200m COP26 Congo basin pledge -- about 20% -- with the pledge's deadline of 2029 meaning delivery is now further imperiled by the latest budget cuts.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Quantifies the shortfall between pledged and delivered climate finance, a benchmark investors can use to discount the credibility of future donor-country forest/climate pledges.

insights.claim: Two of the UK's largest remaining Congo forest projects are also at risk: one aiming to 'incubate forest-friendly enterprises' in the DRC faces reduced budgets, while officials on another project warn it may be forced to operate in fewer countries as cuts take effect.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Signals further downside risk beyond already-announced cuts, suggesting the UK's effective Congo forest-finance contribution could shrink even further from the already-reduced £18.8m baseline.

insights.claim: An official UK government assessment warned that 'degradation or collapse' of the Amazon or Congo rainforests would 'threaten UK national security and prosperity,' framing rainforest protection as a strategic rather than purely environmental priority even as funding is cut.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Highlights an internal policy contradiction -- the UK's own risk assessment labels rainforest collapse a national-security threat while simultaneously cutting the funding meant to prevent it, a governance-risk signal for UK climate policy credibility.

insights.claim: The Congo basin is the planet's largest forested carbon sink, but its six host nations are among the world's poorest and face significant structural funding barriers, including 'complex political economies' and a military coup in Gabon that complicated project delivery.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Identifies political instability as a compounding delivery risk for Congo basin conservation finance, separate from and additive to donor-country budget cuts.

insights.claim: Former Conservative COP26 minister Zac Goldsmith says the UK has 'discarded' its regional rainforest pledges, arguing the UK has gone from being a leader on international nature protection to breaking promises to countries for whom the environment is an existential issue.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: A former-insider's public criticism signals reputational risk to UK diplomatic credibility on climate finance, which could reduce the UK's negotiating leverage at future COPs (e.g., COP31) and reduce trust among tropical forest-nation partners.
insights.quote: We have gone from being perhaps the leader on protecting nature internationally to breaking promises to countries around the world for whom the environment is an existential issue.

insights.claim: WWF International's Damian Fleming warns that tropical forest countries make long-term policy and development choices based on the expectation that international partners will honour their funding commitments -- implying the UK's retreat undermines trust-based conservation diplomacy.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Frames donor-country funding reliability as a structural input into recipient-country forest policy decisions -- a reason to expect weaker future forest-protection commitments from Congo basin nations if donor funding proves unreliable.
insights.quote: Tropical forest countries are making long-term policy and development choices in expectation that international partners will honour their commitments.

insights.claim: MP Sarah Champion, chair of the international development committee, says the UK minister characterized the move away from 'ring-fenced' nature and forest sub-pledges as a 'gamble,' a description Champion said concerned her.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Direct parliamentary confirmation that the UK government itself views dropping dedicated nature/forest funding guarantees as a risky bet rather than a safe policy choice.
insights.quote: When the minister recently appeared before the international development committee, I was concerned to hear her characterise this shift as a 'gamble.'

insights.claim: The UK Labour government says it has met and exceeded its £3bn 'nature' and £1.5bn 'forests' sub-goals within its five-year £11.6bn climate finance target expiring this year, but has dropped these ring-fenced sub-amounts from its new post-2026 climate finance pledge.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: The removal of ring-fenced nature/forest sub-targets from the UK's next climate-finance pledge signals reduced future visibility and predictability for forest-focused funding, even though the current cycle's targets were technically met.

insights.claim: The UK says it supported the creation of Brazil's flagship Tropical Forest Forever Facility (TFFF) but has so far provided no funding for it.
insights.speaker: Josh Gabbatiss
insights.so_what: Another concrete example of UK rhetorical support for forest-finance mechanisms without capital follow-through -- a pattern investors should factor into expectations for actual TFFF funding levels.
